trajectory_summary: This transcript is not analyzable as a sales call. Only one speaker is audible, all customer lines are absent, and the rep's fragments are too sparse and decontextualized to reconstruct call shape, energy arc, or outcome. The v1 outcome tag of 'unknown' is correct — no trajectory verdict is possible from this material.
outcome_dispute: The v1 'unknown' tag is accurate. No appointment, no booking, no DQ, and no customer state can be confirmed from this transcript. The call may have been a complete, successful setter interaction — or a dropped call — the transcript cannot distinguish between them.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Transcript quality — audio re-pull required before coaching
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Okay. The moment that is the text dock key. It says, Randy Con.
primary_coaching_focus.why: This call cannot be coached from the transcript alone. The Deepgram transcription has produced fragments that are too garbled and one-sided to support any grounded claim about rep behavior, customer state, or call outcome. The manager's first action is to pull the original audio recording. Every coaching observation in this brief is conditional on that re-pull. Using this transcript in a 1-on-1 without the audio risks coaching the rep on artifacts, not behavior.

key_moments.quote: Then go to direction West Market in Chicago, Illinois.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the most coherent multi-fragment sequence in the transcript and is the strongest candidate for a verify.address_homeowner_confirmed beat — the rep appears to be confirming or reading back a street address in Chicago. Strategic read: the rep is executing protocol-compliant address confirmation, which is the correct first verify item. Reactive read: the rep is navigating or directing the customer somewhere unrelated to qualification. The strategic read is more consistent with the verify sequence structure, but the one-sided transcript prevents confirmation. Do not dismiss this as noise — it may be the only evidence of protocol adherence in the call.

This fragment is genuinely ambiguous between two distinct verify items: (1) roof type capture — 'singles' as in asphalt shingles, consistent with verify.roof_type_age; (2) household/title status — 'singles' as in single occupant or single owner, consistent with verify.title_co_owners. Both interpretations are equally plausible from the fragment alone. The double confirmation at 02:21 ('Singles. Okay. Perfect.') suggests the rep is echoing back a customer answer, which is consistent with verify behavior, but does not resolve the ambiguity.

key_moments.quote: bill the Comet, but I said to kilowatt hours.
key_moments.stewart_read: This fragment — almost certainly a transcription artifact — appears to reference the utility bill and kilowatt hours, consistent with the bill_collection beat. The rep may be walking the customer through locating the kWh graph on their bill, which is the correct bill_collection template behavior. However, the garbled transcription makes it impossible to assess whether the rep captured the bill, flipped it emotionally, or simply collected it as data and moved on. The coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring failure pattern ('ask + acknowledge + move on' instead of 'ask + acknowledge + flip') cannot be confirmed or denied.

key_moments.quote: Okay. The moment that is the text dock key. It says, Randy Con.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is almost certainly a Deepgram transcription failure of a utility app navigation instruction — 'the text dock key' and 'Randy Con' are likely garbled versions of app UI labels or a customer name. The fragment is consistent with the rep walking a customer through their utility account online, which maps to bill_collection.alternative_paths.shopping_customer_protocol or standard online bill retrieval. No coaching is possible from this fragment as transcribed.
